title: Project Completion Day
nceaFocus: AS92028: Full project completion — applying all achievement criteria from Achieved through to Excellence in a complete financial decision-making report
objectives: Complete a full AS92028-aligned financial decision-making report for a chosen NZ organisation
Apply the six-part report structure with full putake integration at every level
Conduct peer review using the specific AS92028 achievement criteria

vocabulary.definition: The process of reading and evaluating someone else's work using a set of criteria. In NCEA, peer review helps you see your own work more clearly by understanding what markers are looking for.
The specific requirements that must be met to achieve each grade level. In AS92028, the criteria state exactly what must be present for Achieved, Merit, and Excellence.
Marking up your own or someone else's work to show where specific criteria are met. For example, highlighting a putake discussion and writing 'putake — Achieved criterion' next to it.

activities.title: Full Project Work Session — Your Financial Decision-Making Report
activities.setup:
You have been building your AS92028 project across this module. Your organisation might be a sports club, a school canteen, a marae, a community centre, a small café, or any other NZ-grounded organisation you chose. Today's session is for completing your full report. Below is a full project brief to use if you have not yet selected an organisation, or as a reference model if you are working on your own scenario.

Project brief (use this if you need one): Aroha and her whanau run a small café in Ōtara, South Auckland called Kahua Café. The café's putake is to serve their community with affordable, high-quality kai that celebrates Pacific and Māori food culture, and to provide employment for local young people. Kahua has been operating for two years. Current annual income: food sales $148,000, drinks $32,000, catering bookings $18,000. Total income: $198,000. Current annual costs: food supplies $68,000, drinks supplies $11,000, rent $36,000, wages (3 full-time, 1 part-time) $78,000, utilities $8,400, insurance $3,200, marketing $2,400. Total costs: $207,000. Annual deficit: -$9,000. Reserves (savings): $21,600. The café faces a financial issue.

Aroha is considering three options:
Option A: Reduce opening hours from 6 days to 5 days (close Mondays), saving approximately $11,200 per year in wages and utilities.
Option B: Apply for the Māori and Pacific Business Development Fund grant of up to $15,000 (a real MBIE programme) to upgrade kitchen equipment, reducing food preparation time and allowing catering capacity to increase. Expected catering income increase: $12,000 per year. Grant application takes 8 weeks.
Option C: Raise all menu prices by an average of 8%, expected to increase food and drink income by approximately $14,400 per year but at risk of reducing customer numbers by 10 to 15%.
activities.challenge:
Using the six-part structure from Lesson 6, write your complete financial decision-making report. Your report must include:

1. Organisation, putake, and type of financial situation (Achieved)
2. A financial tool comparing at least two options with actual dollar figures (Achieved)
3. A clear recommended decision connected to putake (Achieved)
4. Two stakeholder perspectives with full explanation (Merit)
5. Two consequences — one financial, one non-financial (Merit)
6. Analysis of the extent to which your recommended decision considers stakeholder perspectives (Excellence)
7. Analysis of how the organisation could respond to a consequence in a way that upholds putake (Excellence)
8. A conclusion that ties financial evidence and putake together (Excellence)

If you are using the Kahua Café scenario, calculate the following before writing:
- What is the current annual deficit?
- Under Option A, what is the projected new annual position?
- Under Option B, what is the projected new annual position after Year 1 (assuming the grant is received)?
- Under Option C, what is the best-case and worst-case projected annual position?
hints: For Option A: Costs reduce by $11,200. New costs: $195,800. New position: $198,000 - $195,800 = $2,200 surplus.
For Option B: Income increases by $12,000 from catering. New income: $210,000. Costs stay at $207,000 (the grant funds equipment capital cost, not ongoing costs). New position: $3,000 surplus.

For Option C: Best case (only 10% customer reduction): Income increases by $14,400 but reduces by 10% of $180,000 food and drinks income = $18,000. Net change: -$3,600. New deficit: -$12,600. Worst case (15% reduction): Income increases by $14,400 but reduces by 15% of $180,000 = $27,000. Net change: -$12,600. New deficit: -$21,600.

For putake: The café's putake centres on community service, cultural celebration, and local employment. Option A reduces community service (closed Mondays). Option B grows capacity without raising prices or cutting services. Option C risks pricing out the community the café was built to serve.
activities.solution:
Full sample report for Kahua Café:

Part 1 — Organisation, Putake, and Situation:
Kahua Café is a small café in Ōtara, South Auckland. Their putake is to serve the community with affordable, high-quality kai celebrating Pacific and Māori food culture, and to provide employment for local young people. Kahua faces a financial issue: annual income of $198,000 is less than annual costs of $207,000, resulting in an annual deficit of $9,000. This is a financial issue — the café is operational but losing money, and without a decision, their reserves of $21,600 will be exhausted within approximately 2.4 years.

Part 2 — Financial Tool and Recommended Decision:
Projected annual financial position by option:

Current: Income $198,000, Costs $207,000, Deficit: -$9,000

Option A (reduce to 5 days):
Income: $198,000 (estimated unchanged)
Costs: $207,000 - $11,200 = $195,800
Projected surplus: $2,200

Option B (MBIE grant + catering expansion):
Income: $198,000 + $12,000 = $210,000
Costs: $207,000 (grant covers equipment capital)
Projected surplus: $3,000
Grant received: $15,000 one-off for equipment

Option C (8% price increase):
Best case (10% customer reduction): Net income change = +$14,400 - $18,000 = -$3,600. New deficit: -$12,600
Worst case (15% customer reduction): Net income change = +$14,400 - $27,000 = -$12,600. New deficit: -$21,600

Recommended decision: Kahua should pursue Option B. It generates the highest projected surplus ($3,000), receives a $15,000 capital grant that improves kitchen infrastructure, and grows income without raising prices or reducing service days — directly serving putake. Option A solves the financial problem but closes the café on Mondays, reducing community access. Option C is high risk: even in the best case it worsens the deficit.

Part 3 — Stakeholder Perspectives:
Stakeholder 1 — Regular Ōtara community customers: These customers, many of whom are Pacific and Māori families, visit Kahua because it offers culturally relevant, affordable food. They would support Option B because it maintains current pricing and keeps the café open six days a week. Their concern with Option C would be sharp: an 8% price increase could price some regular customers out, which directly conflicts with the café's commitment to affordability. Their concern with Option A is similar — losing Monday access means one less day of affordable kai in a community where food security matters.

Stakeholder 2 — The café's young local employees: The four staff members — particularly the part-time worker — would be deeply affected by Option A. Reducing to five days likely means reducing the part-time worker's hours or making a staff member redundant. This directly contradicts the café's putake of providing employment for local young people. Under Option B, no jobs are lost; in fact, increased catering bookings may create additional casual work. Option C does not directly threaten jobs but if customer numbers fall significantly, wage costs become unsustainable and redundancies may follow.

Part 4 — Consequences:
Financial consequence of Option B: The $15,000 MBIE grant funds equipment that permanently reduces food preparation time. If catering capacity increases as projected, income from catering grows from $18,000 to $30,000 per year — a 67% increase. This turns the $9,000 annual deficit into a $3,000 surplus within Year 1.

Non-financial consequence of Option B: The 8-week grant application process means the café continues to operate at a deficit for approximately two months while the application is assessed. If any unexpected cost arises during this period, the café would need to draw on its $21,600 reserves. This is manageable given current reserve levels but means the team needs to be disciplined about non-essential spending during the application period.

Part 5 — Excellence Analysis:
Option B strongly considers the perspectives of community customers by maintaining current prices and service days. The café's commitment to affordability and accessibility is fully preserved, and the cultural mission continues without compromise.

Option B strongly considers the perspectives of local employees. No hours are cut, no jobs are lost, and the growth in catering capacity may create additional employment opportunities. This is the most direct expression of the café's putake in employment terms.

Option B partially considers the perspective of existing catering clients who rely on Kahua for bookings. Expanding capacity is good for them, but the 8-week application period may delay the equipment upgrade. The café could partially mitigate this by communicating the upcoming expansion to existing catering clients, managing their expectations and retaining their loyalty during the transition.

Overall, Option B considers the perspectives of all major stakeholders strongly or partially — significantly better than either alternative.

Part 6 — Responding to a Consequence in a Way That Upholds Putake:
The most significant negative consequence of Option B is the 8-week period of continued deficit during grant assessment. To uphold putake — maintaining employment and community access — Aroha should establish a clear spending protocol: defer non-essential purchases, actively promote catering services to existing clients, and consider a small community 'Support Kahua' campaign on social media explaining the expansion plan. Many Ōtara community members have a strong connection to a business that celebrates their culture — inviting them into the story of Kahua's growth is consistent with the café's putake and may generate additional customer visits during the most financially constrained period.

Conclusion:
Kahua Café should pursue Option B. It is the only option that resolves the financial issue, grows the business, preserves the café's affordability, and protects local employment. Options A and C solve the numbers problem but do so at the cost of what Kahua actually exists for. Option B lets the numbers and the values work together. That is what good financial decision-making looks like.

activities.title: Peer Review Using AS92028 Criteria
activities.prompt:
Exchange your completed report with a classmate. Using the criteria below, annotate their report by placing a tick and the level label (A, M, or E) next to each element you can find:

Achieved criteria (A): Describes the financial need/issue/opportunity. Uses a financial tool with actual dollar amounts. States a recommended decision. Discusses putake.

Merit criteria (M): Explains at least two stakeholder perspectives. Explains possible consequences for the organisation and stakeholders.

Excellence criteria (E): Analyses the extent to which the decision considers stakeholder perspectives. Analyses how the organisation could respond to consequences in a way that upholds putake.

After annotating, write two pieces of specific feedback: one thing that is working particularly well, and one specific change that would improve the response. Be precise — 'it needs more detail' is not useful feedback. 'The stakeholder perspective for employees does not explain why the wage reduction matters to them personally — add a sentence about living costs or financial security' is useful feedback.
activities.nceaLink: This peer review exercise uses the actual AS92028 achievement criteria as a marking guide. Understanding what a marker looks for at each level is one of the most powerful ways to improve your own writing. If you can identify where criteria are met in someone else's work, you can identify where they are missing in your own.
